Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered this semester. I'd try to set aside time in the afternoons, but with my extracurriculars and everything else going on, I'd often end up studying in bursts whenever I could fit it in. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd listen to lecture recordings while doing other things, which helped me feel like I was keeping up even when I wasn't sitting at my desk. I aimed for consistency but life kept getting in the way.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot based on what was due and what else I had going on. Some weeks I'd be really on top of things, logging in regularly and watching videos. Other weeks, especially when my clubs had events or I had personal stuff happening, I'd barely touch the course materials until something was due. I kept telling myself I'd get into a better rhythm, but it never quite stuck.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline coming up or getting a notification that something new was posted. Sometimes my study group would mention something in our group chat and I'd hop on to check it out. Peer support was huge for me—when my friends were active, I felt more motivated to log in too. Without those external prompts, I could go days without thinking about the LMS.
Q4: There was a brief period early in the semester when I made it a habit to check the LMS every morning with my coffee. That lasted maybe three weeks before things got busy. After that, it became more reactive—I'd log in when I needed something specific rather than as part of a routine. I wish I'd been able to maintain that early momentum, but it just didn't stick.
Q5: The video lectures were honestly my lifeline because I learn best by listening. I'd watch them, sometimes multiple times, and really absorb the material that way. The discussion boards were also helpful when people were actually engaging—I had some great exchanges with classmates that helped clarify concepts. When peers were active and explaining things in their own words, it clicked better for me than reading the textbook.
Q6: I mostly ignored the reading materials and some of the supplementary PDFs. I know they were probably useful, but reading dense text just doesn't work for my brain the way listening does. I'd download them with good intentions but they'd sit unopened on my laptop. The practice quizzes I also put off because they felt optional, even though I know now they would have helped me prepare for exams.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see an immediate connection to what I needed to know or when it involved interaction with other people. If I could discuss something with classmates or if a video directly addressed something I was confused about, I was all in. Things that felt like busywork or that I couldn't connect to the bigger picture got pushed to the bottom of my priority list.
Q8: Optional activities felt like suggestions I could get to 'later'—and later often never came. Required ones had deadlines attached, so they got done, even if sometimes at the last minute. There's something about knowing something counts toward my grade that makes me prioritize it differently. Optional stuff, even if it was genuinely helpful, just couldn't compete with everything else demanding my attention.
Q9: Oh absolutely. I downloaded a ton of lecture recordings and listened to them on my phone while commuting or working out. I also had a study group that met in person, and we'd work through problems together using our notes and sometimes YouTube videos. A lot of my actual learning happened in those face-to-face sessions with my peers, not in front of my computer logged into the LMS.
Q10: My study group meetings are the perfect example. We'd meet for hours, going over material, quizzing each other, explaining concepts out loud. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. Also, I'd re-listen to downloaded lectures multiple times—the LMS only knew I watched them once. My best studying happened when I was talking things through with friends, not clicking through modules.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download the lecture videos and audio files, then access them offline constantly. The LMS would show one view, but I probably listened to some lectures four or five times while doing other things. Same with any slides or notes—I'd save them locally and review them on my tablet without being connected. It was just more convenient that way.
Q12: Around midterms, everything piled up at once—my extracurriculars had major events, I had assignments in multiple courses, and I just felt stretched thin. Even though my stress levels weren't terrible, I felt this moderate anxiety about falling behind that made it hard to focus. I'd sit down to study and my mind would be elsewhere. That's when my attendance and engagement really dropped.
Q13: The workload from other courses and my extracurricular commitments definitely affected how much I used the LMS. When I was overwhelmed, the LMS became just another thing on my list rather than a learning tool. Fatigue was real too—after a long day, the last thing I wanted was to log in and watch another lecture. I'd tell myself I'd catch up on the weekend, but weekends filled up fast.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd be logged in, video playing, but I was really just going through the motions. My mind would be on something else entirely, or I'd be half-watching while scrolling my phone. I'd technically complete the activity but couldn't tell you what I learned. It felt like I was checking a box rather than actually engaging with the material.
Q15: I definitely postponed the assignments more than I should have. Even when I knew they were important, I'd convince myself I worked better under pressure or that I'd understand the material better if I waited. Part of it was anxiety about not doing well, so avoiding it felt easier in the moment. My assignment completion rate suffered because of this pattern.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the main thing driving my LMS usage. I'd have low activity for days, then suddenly be very active right before something was due. It wasn't the healthiest approach, but that urgency was what got me to actually sit down and engage. Without a deadline looming, I struggled to prioritize course work over everything else in my life.
Q17: When I got feedback, especially positive feedback or constructive comments, it motivated me to engage more. Knowing someone was actually looking at my work made it feel more worthwhile. But feedback often came late, so by the time I got it, I'd already moved on mentally. Quick feedback would have helped me stay more consistently engaged throughout the course.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which I thought I'd love, but it actually made it easier for me to fall behind. Without strict weekly requirements, I could always tell myself I'd catch up later. The modules were well-organized, but the flexibility meant I could—and did—procrastinate. More structured check-ins might have kept me on track.
Q19: The video content being available for download made engagement easier since I could learn on my own schedule and in my preferred way. But the sheer number of online courses I was taking—eight total—made it hard to give this one the attention it deserved. The course itself wasn't poorly designed; I was just spread too thin across too many platforms and commitments.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me, actually—logging in, watching videos, browsing materials, but then running out of time or energy to actually complete and submit assignments. You can be genuinely trying to learn and engage with content but still struggle to produce finished work. Life gets in the way, or anxiety about doing it perfectly leads to not doing it at all.
Q21: That makes total sense to me. If someone is studying effectively offline—using downloaded materials, study groups, textbooks—their LMS activity would be low but their understanding could be solid. I did a lot of learning outside the system. My exam score wasn't great, but my assignment scores when I did submit were decent, which shows I understood the material when I engaged with it properly.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend on things. But they miss the quality of that engagement and all the learning that happens offline. They can't capture a study group conversation or how many times I replayed a downloaded lecture. They show behavior but not understanding or effort that happens away from the screen.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and making an effort to understand it, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm asking questions, discussing with peers, thinking about how concepts connect. It's not about how many times I logged in—it's about whether my brain was actually working on the material. Some of my most engaged moments happened nowhere near the LMS.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my peer support network meant to my learning. Those conversations with classmates, the study sessions, the way we helped each other understand difficult concepts—that was where real engagement happened for me. It also wouldn't show that despite my low attendance and completion rates, I was genuinely trying and cared about learning. My circumstances made it hard, but I never stopped wanting to succeed.
